rationale: Infection audit directly targets hospital-acquired infections — core to patient safety.
Incident reporting system is a foundational patient safety mechanism.
JCI accreditation directly measures and enforces clinical care quality standards.
Specialist nurse training improves clinical competency and care delivery quality.
More specialist consultants directly improves the quality of patient care.
EHR enables safer medication management and reduces clinical errors.
Elderly care unit directly delivers patient care, though specialised rather than safety-focused.
Land acquisition is an infrastructure decision with no direct impact on care quality.
Construction planning does not address patient safety processes.
Medical tourism is a revenue strategy, not a patient safety initiative.
Procurement cost reduction has no meaningful link to clinical care quality.
A staff sports day has no impact on patient safety outcomes.
Land acquisition is the essential first step for the hospital capacity expansion.
Building the 75-bed expansion wing directly delivers the capacity increase objective.
Opening an elderly care unit adds a new specialty ward, expanding service capacity.
Medical tourism requires adequate bed capacity and new service lines.
Accreditation can attract more patients but does not directly expand infrastructure.
Infection audits address care quality, not physical capacity expansion.
Incident reporting does not contribute to increasing bed capacity.
Nurse training improves skills but does not expand physical infrastructure.
An EHR system supports administration but does not increase bed capacity.
Sports day has no relation to hospital capacity expansion.
A specialist nursing training programme directly builds clinical workforce skills.
A retention incentive scheme directly targets the goal of retaining skilled staff.
Recruiting 10 specialist consultants directly expands the skilled medical workforce.
JCI accreditation requires staff training and drives a culture of professional development.
Running infection audits requires trained staff but does not build workforce capacity itself.
Land acquisition is a property transaction with no workforce development component.
Construction management is unrelated to recruiting or retaining clinical staff.
Medical tourism is a revenue initiative, not a workforce development programme.
Procurement renegotiation targets supply costs and has no workforce development angle.
A sports day boosts morale but is not a structured workforce development initiative.
Deploying an EHR system is the primary action for modernising the hospital's digital systems.
Automating scheduling and billing directly modernises administrative digital workflows.
Cybersecurity and data backup upgrades are core digital infrastructure requirements.
JCI accreditation requires digital documentation but is primarily a quality standard.
Modern consultants may bring digital expectations but recruitment is not a tech initiative.
Infection control audits are clinical processes, not technology modernisation.
A digital incident reporting tool has a technology component but is safety-focused.
Land acquisition is property development with no digital technology component.
Construction of a hospital wing is not a digital or technology initiative.
Medical tourism relies on digital marketing but is primarily a revenue strategy.
A sports day has no digital or technology modernisation element.
Medical tourism packages directly grow revenue from a new international patient segment.
Renegotiating supplier contracts directly reduces operating costs and improves margins.
The expansion wing is a large capital investment expected to generate future revenue growth.
Automating billing accelerates revenue collection and reduces administrative overhead costs.
Elderly care unit is a new revenue-generating service line supporting financial growth.
JCI accreditation can attract premium patients but is primarily a quality initiative.
EHR reduces administrative waste and can lower operational costs indirectly.
Infection audits are a compliance cost, not a revenue or financial sustainability measure.
Staff training is a cost investment; its financial return is indirect and long-term.
Retention schemes cost money upfront; financial benefit via lower turnover is indirect.
Cybersecurity is a necessary cost but does not directly generate revenue or cut major costs.
A staff sports day is a morale expense with no financial sustainability impact.
